Introduction
In Python, everything is an object — integers, strings, lists, even functions and classes themselves. This means every value you work with has an identity, a type, and a value. Understanding how Python manages these objects in memory is one of the most important concepts for writing correct, efficient Python code. In this post, we’ll explore what it really means for an object to be mutable or immutable, how Python stores them, and what happens when you pass them to functions.
id and type
Every object in Python has two fundamental properties you can inspect: its type and its identity.
type() tells you what kind of object something is:
python>>> a = 89
>>> type(a)
<class ‘int’>
>>> b = [1, 2, 3]
>>> type(b)
<class ‘list’>
id() returns the memory address of the object in CPython — a unique integer that identifies where the object lives in memory during its lifetime:
python>>> a = “hello”
>>> id(a)
140234567891234
>>> b = [1, 2, 3]
>>> id(b)
140234567891456
The is operator compares two objects by identity (same id), while == compares by value:
python>>> l1 = [1, 2, 3]
>>> l2 = [1, 2, 3]
>>> l1 == l2 # same value
True
>>> l1 is l2 # different objects in memory
False
Mutable Objects
A mutable object is one whose value can be changed after it is created — without creating a new object. The object stays at the same memory address, but its contents change.
The mutable built-in types in Python are:
list
dict
set
bytearray
python>>> l = [1, 2, 3]
>>> id(l)
140234567891456
>>> l.append(4)
>>> l
[1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> id(l)
140234567891456 # same address! object was modified in place
Memory diagram:
Before append: After append:
l ──► [1, 2, 3] l ──► [1, 2, 3, 4]
(addr: 456) (addr: 456) ← same object
Because lists are mutable, aliases (two variables pointing to the same list) can lead to surprising behaviour:
python>>> l1 = [1, 2, 3]
>>> l2 = l1 # l2 is an alias of l1
>>> l1.append(4)
>>> l2 # l2 sees the change!
[1, 2, 3, 4]
Memory diagram:
l1 ──┐
▼
[1, 2, 3, 4]
▲
l2 ──┘
Both l1 and l2 point to the same object. This is called aliasing.
Immutable Objects
An immutable object cannot be changed after it is created. Any operation that appears to “modify” it actually creates a brand new object in memory.
The immutable built-in types in Python are:
int, float, complex
str
tuple
frozenset
bytes
python>>> s = “hello”
>>> id(s)
140234567891234
>>> s += “ world”
>>> id(s)
140234567899999 # NEW address — a new string was created
Memory diagram:
Before: After s += “ world”:
s ──► “hello” s ──► “hello world” (new object)
(addr: 234) (addr: 999)
“hello” still exists in memory
until garbage collected
For integers:
python>>> a = 89
>>> id(a)
140234567800000
>>> a += 1
>>> id(a)
140234567800028 # different object — 90 is a different int
Special case: Tuples and Frozen Sets
Tuples and frozen sets are immutable — you cannot add or remove elements. But they can contain mutable objects:
python>>> t = ([1, 2], [3, 4])
>>> t[0].append(99) # modifying the list INSIDE the tuple
>>> t
([1, 2, 99], [3, 4])
The tuple itself didn’t change (same elements at same positions), but the mutable object it contains was modified. This is an important nuance — immutability applies to the container structure, not necessarily to what’s inside it.
Why Does It Matter? How Python Treats Them Differently
Because immutable objects cannot change, Python can safely reuse them. Two variables can point to the same immutable object with no risk — neither can change the shared object.
python>>> a = “hello”
>>> b = “hello”
>>> a is b
True # CPython reuses the same string object (string interning)
Python automatically interns (reuses) short strings and small integers for efficiency. Mutable objects like lists are never shared this way — each [1, 2, 3] literal creates a new list.
Integer Pre-allocation: NSMALLPOSINTS and NSMALLNEGINTS
CPython pre-allocates the 262 most commonly used integers at startup, before any user code runs. This range is:
NSMALLNEGINTS: integers from -5 down to -5
NSMALLPOSINTS: integers from 0 up to 256
python>>> a = 5
>>> b = 5
>>> a is b
True # same pre-allocated object
>>> a = 1000
>>> b = 1000
>>> a is b
False # outside the cached range, two separate objects
These specific values (-5 to 256) were chosen because they are the integers that appear most frequently in real programs — loop counters, array indices, small arithmetic results. Pre-allocating them avoids the overhead of creating and destroying thousands of tiny integer objects during normal execution.
How Arguments Are Passed to Functions
Python passes arguments by assignment — sometimes called “pass by object reference.” When you call a function, the parameter name inside the function becomes a new reference to the same object the caller passed.
With immutable objects (integers, strings, tuples):
pythondef increment(n):
n += 1
a = 1
increment(a)
print(a) # still 1 — n was rebound to a new int, a was unaffected
Memory diagram:
Before call: Inside function: After call:
a ──► 1 n ──► 1 a ──► 1 (unchanged)
n += 1
n ──► 2 (new object)
The function cannot modify the caller’s a because integers are immutable. n += 1 creates a new integer 2 and rebinds n to it — a is untouched.
With mutable objects (lists, dicts):
pythondef append_item(lst):
lst.append(4)
l = [1, 2, 3]
append_item(l)
print(l) # [1, 2, 3, 4] — the original list was modified!
Memory diagram:
Before call: Inside function:
l ──► [1,2,3] lst ──► [1,2,3] (same object!)
lst.append(4)
lst ──► [1,2,3,4]
After call:
l ──► [1,2,3,4] ← l sees the change
Because lst and l point to the same list object, mutations inside the function are visible outside it.
However, reassigning the parameter inside the function does NOT affect the caller:
pythondef assign_value(n, v):
n = v # rebinds n locally, doesn’t touch l1
l1 = [1, 2, 3]
l2 = [4, 5, 6]
assign_value(l1, l2)
print(l1) # [1, 2, 3] — unchanged
n = v just makes the local name n point to l2. It doesn’t change what l1 points to in the caller.
Summary
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Understanding mutability is fundamental to debugging Python programs — especially when functions seem to unexpectedly modify their inputs, or when you expect two variables to be independent but they’re not.
Published by Marvella Munezero — ALU Software Engineering student
